<div class="blog-header">

# Consumer Surplus: Getting More Than You Paid For

<div class="blog-subtitle">

People are willing to pay more than what companies charge

</div>

<div class="podcast-container style-1">

<div class="audio-player-container">

<div class="controls">

<a href="#" class="not-smooth play-pause-btn"></a>

<div class="podcast-links">

<div class="dropdown">

<div class="dropdown-menu" aria-labelledby="podcast-open-link">

</div>

<div class="date">

Jul 25 2019

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="content">

**Naval:** [Consumer surplus and producer
surplus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic_surplus) are important
concepts. Consumer surplus is the excess value you get from something
when you pay less than you were willing to pay.

I get a lot of joy out of my morning Starbucks coffee. Obviously I’ve
made some money. So if my coffee cost \$20, I would pay it.

But Starbucks doesn’t know that. They can’t price the coffee at \$20
just for me, because they’re selling the exact same product to others.
So I’m getting a lot of consumer surplus out of the coffee.

<span id="more-28493335"></span>

**Consumer surplus is the extra value you get when you pay less than you
were willing**

All businesses generate consumer surplus. It’s a good thing to keep in
mind when someone’s harping on about how evil companies are. Amazon
might be a trillion-dollar company, but I’ll bet they’re generating
trillions of dollars in consumer surplus through people’s willingness to
pay for convenience. A lot of people are willing to pay more than what
Amazon charges.
